title: MarkovTier: Anticipatory Page Migration via Markov Phase Models for Tiered Memory Systems
description: We propose MarkovTier, a page migration framework for tiered memory systems (DRAM + CXL) that models workload phase transitions as a Markov chain and proactively migrates pages before phase transitions occur, reducing the migration lag that degrades performance in all existing reactive systems.
motivation: All existing tiered memory page migration policies (TPP, Nomad, ALTO, ARMS, ArtMem, FlexMem) are fundamentally reactive: they detect hot/cold pages after access patterns change and then migrate. This causes a 'migration lag' during phase transitions where newly-hot pages are stuck in slow memory. Programs exhibit well-documented, often predictable phase behavior (Sherwood et al., ASPLOS'02), yet no tiered memory system exploits this predictability for proactive migration. This gap results in significant performance loss during phase transitions, particularly for workloads with distinct phases and limited fast-tier capacity.
proposed_approach: MarkovTier operates in three stages: (1) Online Phase Detection using working set signatures (compact hash-based fingerprints of accessed pages) to identify execution phases and transitions; (2) Markov Transition Learning that maintains a phase transition probability matrix updated online via exponential moving averages; (3) Anticipatory Migration that, when a phase transition is predicted with sufficient confidence, proactively migrates the predicted next phase's working set pages to fast memory before the transition occurs. A confidence-weighted migration rate and bandwidth budget mechanism prevent misprediction costs from exceeding benefits.

related_work.title: TPP: Transparent Page Placement for CXL-Enabled Tiered-Memory
related_work.authors: Hasan Al Maruf, Hao Wang, Abhishek Dhanotia, Johannes Weiner, Niket Agarwal, Pallab Bhattacharya, Chris Petersen, Mosharaf Chowdhury, Shobhit Kanaujia, Prakash Chauhan
related_work.relation: Baseline reactive policy using NUMA hints for promotion and reclamation for demotion, integrated into Linux kernel. Does not consider phase behavior.

related_work.title: Tiered Memory Management Beyond Hotness
related_work.authors: Jinshu Liu, Hamid Hadian, Hanchen Xu, Huaicheng Li
related_work.relation: State-of-the-art reactive system using AOL metric and migration throttling (ALTO). Still reactive; does not predict or anticipate phase changes.

related_work.title: Nomad: Non-Exclusive Memory Tiering via Transactional Page Migration
related_work.authors: Lingfeng Xiang, Zhen Lin, Weishu Deng, Hui Lu, Jia Rao, Yifan Yuan, Ren Wang
related_work.relation: Reduces migration cost via non-exclusive shadow copies but does not address migration timing relative to phases.

related_work.title: ARMS: Adaptive and Robust Memory Tiering System
related_work.authors: Sujay Yadalam, Konstantinos Kanellis, Michael Swift, Shivaram Venkataraman
related_work.relation: Closest to our work; uses change-point detection on bandwidth to detect phase shifts. However, ARMS reacts to changes rather than predicting them, and does not model transition probabilities.

related_work.title: ArtMem: Adaptive Migration in Reinforcement Learning-Enabled Tiered Memory
related_work.authors: Xinyue Yi, Hongchao Du, Yu Wang, Jie Zhang, Qiao Li, Chun Jason Xue
related_work.relation: Uses Q-learning for adaptive migration. RL agent implicitly captures some phase behavior but does not explicitly model phase transitions or perform anticipatory migration.

related_work.title: FlexMem: Adaptive Page Profiling and Migration for Tiered Memory
related_work.authors: Dong Xu, Junhee Ryu, Jinho Baek, Kwangsik Shin, Pengfei Su, Dong Li
related_work.relation: Adapts profiling methods to phase changes but profiling adaptation is reactive, not predictive.

related_work.title: Automatically Characterizing Large Scale Program Behavior
related_work.authors: Timothy Sherwood, Erez Perelman, Greg Hamerly, Brad Calder
related_work.relation: Foundational phase detection work using Basic Block Vectors. Established that program phases are pervasive and identifiable. Our working set signatures extend this concept to the memory page level.

related_work.title: MTM: Rethinking Memory Profiling and Migration for Multi-Tiered Large Memory
related_work.authors: Dong Xu, Huaicheng Li, Gregory R. Ganger, Hasan Al Maruf
related_work.relation: Demonstrated that DAMON is slow to detect phase changes (3x slower than MTM). Motivates the need for proactive approaches that avoid detection delay altogether.

related_work.title: Managing Multi-Configuration Hardware via Dynamic Working Set Analysis
related_work.authors: Ashutosh S. Dhodapkar, James E. Smith
related_work.relation: Proposed working set signatures for online phase detection. Our approach extends working set signatures to page-level memory tiering.

hypothesis: Modeling workload phase transitions as a Markov chain and using transition predictions to proactively migrate pages before phase changes can reduce slow-tier memory accesses during transitions by 30-60% compared to reactive migration policies, while incurring at most 5-10% overhead from mispredicted anticipatory migrations.
success_criteria: At least 20% reduction in slow-tier access ratio during phase transitions vs. best reactive baseline
At most 10% increase in total migration data volume
Phase transition prediction accuracy above 70% for workloads with repetitive phase patterns
No significant performance degradation for workloads with unpredictable phase patterns (confidence threshold prevents harmful speculation)
Clear ablation results showing the contribution of each component (phase detection, Markov model, confidence thresholding)

feasibility.compute: All experiments are trace-driven simulation in Python, requiring only CPU. No real tiered memory hardware needed.
feasibility.time: Simulator implementation (~3 hours) + experiments and analysis (~5 hours) = ~8 hours total
feasibility.data: Synthetic workload traces with controlled phase structure, plus traces modeled after published SPEC CPU phase characterizations
